L1.area: TLS connection management and cryptographic data serialization

L2.description: A use-after-free vulnerability exists where X509 certificate objects are created without proper memory ownership. When the underlying memory is freed but the wrapper objects persist, subsequent access results in use-after-free, potentially leading to a denial of service or arbitrary code execution.

L3.area: X509 certificate object construction in TLS verification callbacks and PKCS#12 certificate loading
L3.description: A use-after-free vulnerability exists where X509 certificate objects are constructed from raw pointers without incrementing the reference count or establishing proper garbage collection. In TLS certificate verification callbacks, the certificate obtained from the verification context does not own its memory, so retaining a reference after the callback completes leads to use-after-free. Similarly, when loading PKCS#12 data, individual CA certificates extracted from the certificate stack lack proper memory ownership, causing use-after-free when the stack is freed.

ground_truth.reason: X509 certificate objects are constructed without proper memory ownership in TLS verification callbacks and PKCS#12 loading, allowing use-after-free when the backing memory is freed while wrapper objects persist.
